narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 40.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was the key reason your application was rejected—they use this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar. Your profile shows strong undergraduate performance with a 3.60 GPA, well above the typical student's 3.21 average, along with middle-family income, being a white male planning full-time studies, but it also includes lower rankings in your school's deciles for Year 1 and Year 3 grades (both in the 2nd decile, compared to averages around the 5th or 6th), and a notably low LSAT score of 22.70 versus the usual 36.70. When the model evaluated you, your Year 3 school decile ranking had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward failure because it was so much lower than what it sees in successful bar takers. Next in impact was your LSAT score, which similarly pulled strongly toward a fail prediction due to being well below the norm for those who pass. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, again contributing to the risk because of its low standing relative to peers. Family income bracket, racial background, undergraduate GPA, and full-time status each played smaller but still notable roles in nudging toward failure, while your gender as male had a slight counterbalancing effect toward passing. Overall, these academic performance indicators—especially the low deciles and LSAT—outweighed your GPA strength and made your profile riskier than most applicants who get approved. Compared to typical students in the dataset, where about 56% are male, 84% white, and 93% full-time like you, your standout challenges were those below-average grades and test score, which signal potential hurdles for bar success. To improve your future chances, focus on bolstering test-taking skills, perhaps through targeted LSAT or bar prep courses, since your low score there was a top driver. Building stronger study habits could help replicate your undergraduate GPA success in law school, directly addressing the weak decile rankings from earlier years. Retaking the LSAT if possible or gaining experience in high-stakes testing environments might shift future predictions in your favor. With dedication to these areas, you could align more closely with profiles of bar passers and strengthen your next application. Keep pushing—you've already shown GPA capability that sets you apart.
